coredata.dc:title: Study of the interaction between green Si quantum dots and bovine serum albumin via spectroscopic methods and its effects on antioxidant capacity and esterase activity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ji, Meng
Qiang, Yanan
Li, Si
Tian, Chunyuan
Zhuang, Xuming
Qi, Shengda
Luan, Feng
coredata.dc:description: In this study, the interaction mechanism between Si quantum dots (SiQDs) and bovine serum albumin (BSA), as well as the conformational and functional alterations of BSA, were rigorously investigated via multispectral techniques and dynamic light scattering analysis. van der Waals forces and hydrogen bonding, as well as an exothermic reaction and a decrease in entropy, were the primary forces involved in the binding of SiQDs to BSA. In the binding process, SiQDs exhibit preferential proximity to Site I over other potential binding sites. Furthermore, the incorporation of SiQDs into BSA causes a reduction in the α-helix and β-sheet contents of the protein. This, in turn, leads to an increased degree of stretching and an increase in the hydrophilicity of BSA. Furthermore, the increase in the antioxidant capacity and esterase activity of BSA upon the addition of SiQDs was positively correlated with the concentration. These findings elucidate the underlying interaction mechanism between green fluorescence-emitting SiQDs and BSA and provide fundamental insights into both the biological and toxicological implications of SiQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Si quantum dots (SiQDs)
Bovine serum albumin (BSA)
Interaction mechanisms
Conformational changes
Functional changes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence spectroscopy studies of the interaction between SiQDs and BSA
UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy study of the interaction between SiQDs and BSA
Study of structural changes in the interaction of SiQDs with BSA
Synchronous fluorescence spectroscopy study of the interaction between SiQDs and BSA
Three-dimensional fluorescence spectroscopy study of the interaction between SiQDs and BSA
Circular dichroism (CD) study of the interaction between SiQDs and BSA
Measurement of the hydration diameter and surface charge
Effects of SiQD and BSA interactions on important physiological functions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra of SiQDs under different reaction conditions: (A) Content of catechol; (B) Content of thiourea; (C) Reaction time; (D) Reaction temperature.
(A) Excitation and emission fluorescence spectra of SiQDs; (B) UV–Vis absorption spectra of SiQDs.
Optical stability under different conditions: (A) pH; (B) Placement time; (C) UV lamp irradiation at different durations; (D) NaCl.
Characterization of synthesized SiQDs: (A) HRTEM plots at 10 nm; (B) Particle size distribution plots; (C) FT-IR plots; (D) XRD plots.
XPS spectra of SiQDs: (A) Full XPS spectra of SiQDs; (B) Fine spectra of C1s; (C) Fine spectra of N1s; (D) Fine spectra of O1s; (E) Fine spectra of Si2p.
Fluorescence spectra of BSA at different concentrations of SiQDs.

(A) Stern–Volmer plots of the BSA-SiQDs system; (B) Modified Stern–Volmer plots of the BSA-SiQDs system; (C) Hill plots of the BSA-SiQDs systems at three temperatures; (D) Van’t Hoff plot of the BSA-SiQDs systems.
(A) Fluorescence spectra of ibuprofen on the BSA-SiQDs systems; (B) Fluorescence spectral of warfarin on the BSA-SiQDs systems; (C) Modified Stern–Volmer plots of competition between the two sites.
UV–visible absorption spectra of BSA (A) before and after the addition of SiQDs; (B) at 278 nm with different concentrations of SiQDs.
Synchronized fluorescence spectra of BSA at different concentrations of SiQDs (A) Tyr; (B) Trp.
Three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of BSA at different concentrations of SiQDs (A) 0.0 × 10−4 mol/L; (B) 2.5 × 10−4 mol/L; (C) 7.5 × 10−4 mol/L; (D) 12.5 × 10−4 mol/L.
(A) Circular dichroism spectrum of BSA at different concentrations of SiQDs; (B) Secondary structure percentage content map of BSA.
(A) The hydration diameter of SiQDs, BSA, and BSA-SiQDs systems; (B) Zeta potential of SiQDs, BSA, and BSA-SiQDs systems.
(A) The esterase activity of BSA at different concentrations of SiQDs; (B) The antioxidant capacity of BSA at different concentrations of SiQDs.
Binding constants (Kb ) and binding number (n) of the BSA-SiQDs systems.
Thermodynamic parameters of the BSA-SiQDs systems at different temperatures.
The associative binding constants (Ka ) of three BSA-SiQDs systems.
Three-dimensional fluorescence spectra data of BSA and three BSA-SiQDs systems.

head.ce:title: Study of the interaction between green Si quantum dots and bovine serum albumin via spectroscopic methods and its effects on antioxidant capacity and esterase activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yantai University, Yantai 264005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yantai University, Yantai 264005, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yunnan Institute of Measuring and Testing Technology, Kunming, Yunnan 650100, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Yunnan Institute of Measuring and Testing Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Yunnan Institute of Measuring and Testing Technology, Kunming, Yunnan 650100, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730030, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730030, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, the interaction mechanism between Si quantum dots (SiQDs) and bovine serum albumin (BSA), as well as the conformational and functional alterations of BSA, were rigorously investigated via multispectral techniques and dynamic light scattering analysis. van der Waals forces and hydrogen bonding, as well as an exothermic reaction and a decrease in entropy, were the primary forces involved in the binding of SiQDs to BSA. In the binding process, SiQDs exhibit preferential proximity to Site I over other potential binding sites. Furthermore, the incorporation of SiQDs into BSA causes a reduction in the α-helix and β-sheet contents of the protein. This, in turn, leads to an increased degree of stretching and an increase in the hydrophilicity of BSA. Furthermore, the increase in the antioxidant capacity and esterase activity of BSA upon the addition of SiQDs was positively correlated with the concentration. These findings elucidate the underlying interaction mechanism between green fluorescence-emitting SiQDs and BSA and provide fundamental insights into both the biological and toxicological implications of SiQDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data and materials related to the study are available upon re-quest.

ce:section.ce:para: Silicon-based nanomaterials such as silicon quantum dots (SiQDs), mesoporous silica and other hybrid materials are usually referred to as a variety of nanomaterials that are prepared from silicon as the main component [1,2]. Silicon, which is one of the most abundant elements in the Earth's crust and accounts for 26.4 % of the total amount (only less than oxygen of 49.4 %), can be obtained at relatively low economic costs. Moreover, as an indispensable element for living organisms, silicon plays a crucial role in diverse biological processes. Specifically, silicon contributes significantly to maintaining the elasticity of human vascular tissues, thereby mitigating the risk of arterial sclerosis [3]. Moreover, Si is easily excreted from urine, which provides a basis for the excellent biocompatibility of this type of nanomaterial [3].

SiQDs, silicon-based zero-dimensional nanomaterials with both low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility, as mentioned above, have now been widely used in biomedical fields, including bioimaging, biosensors, and drug delivery [4–6]. However, many challenges remain in biomedical applications. Compared with those of mature IIIA-VA- or IIB-VIA-based nanomaterials, the optical properties of SiQDs obtained via conventional synthesis methods are usually less desirable, thus limiting their practical applications [7]. In the domains of life sciences, chemistry, and materials science, the synergistic effect, encompassing the collaborative interplay of two or more components, is extensively employed. This phenomenon yields an aggregate positive impact that surpasses the individual influence of any solitary component [8–11]. Therefore, in this work, we attempt to synthesize SiQDs with green fluorescence emission via the synergistic effect of catechol and thiourea bi-reducers. The excellent optical properties of these materials make them good materials for biomedical applications.

For biomedical applications, it is very important to analyze the interaction of SiQDs with proteins, especially those in the blood system, since proteins significantly impact the functional performance of SiQDs. Some studies have focused on this particular subject area. Usharani Nagarajan's group used the asymmetric flow field–flow fractionation technique (AF4) with differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) to provide information about the size and shape of fetal bovine serum (FBS)-SiQD complexes, their elution profiles, their real-time interactions with FBS components, and the thermodynamic behavior of proteins in the presence of SiQDs [12]. By using multispectral analysis and molecular modeling techniques, Shanmugavel Chinnathambi and colleagues delved into the conformational changes that occur during the interaction between SiQDs and hemoglobin as well as thrombin. Furthermore, they utilized molecular dynamics simulations to determine the favorable binding affinity of SiQDs for hemoglobin and thrombin proteins [13]. Dongyan Li et al. synthesized SiQDs with amino and imidazole groups and applied them to the detection of bovine hemoglobin, which was shown to have a wide linear range as well as a low detection limit [14]. In addition, Mukherjee performed a spectroscopic investigation of the interaction between bovine serum albumin (BSA) and amine-functionalized SiQDs, providing insights into the binding mechanisms and conformational changes of the system [15].

Bovine serum albumin (BSA), a prevalent model protein, exhibits a high degree of structural homology and amino acid sequence similarity to human serum albumin (HSA), which comprises 583 amino acid residues and has a molecular weight of 66.430 kDa (kDa) [16]. Structurally, BSA consists of an α-helix and an irregularly coiled polypeptide structure, which contains three structural domains: structural domain I (1–193), structural domain II (194–384), and structural domain III (385–583), with two subdomains in each individual domain [17]. In terms of biological functions, BSA not only serves as a carrier for biomolecules such as lipids and hormones but also participates in the regulation of blood pH and osmolality. BSA also plays an important role in biochemical reactions such as the immune response and cell signaling [18].

In the present study, SiQDs with excellent optical properties were synthesized on the basis of the synergistic effect between catechol and thiourea bi-reducers. Then, multispectral approaches and dynamic scattered light techniques [19,20] were used to investigate the interaction mechanism including quenching mechanism of BSA, the number of binding sites and important thermodynamic parameters between BSA and SiQDs. Meanwhile, the antioxidant capacity and esterase activity of BSA upon the addition of SiQDs was studied. This study aims to help us understand the process by which SiQDs bind to BSA and their effect on the molecular structure and functionality. Further, we hope to provide some useful information on the biological and toxicological implications of SiQDs.

Thiourea, catechol, N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl) propyl]-ethylenediamine (DAMO), BSA, ibuprofen and warfarin were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China); p-nitrophenyl acetate (pNPA) and 5,5-dithio-bis-(2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB) were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich (Shanghai, China); and Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals used in this study were of analytical grade, and ultrapure water was used in the experiments. BSA and SiQDs were dissolved in PBS (0.02 mol/L, pH = 7.20–7.40) for subsequent experiments.

The synthesis of SiQDs was performed according to the literature with modifications [21–23].First, different contents of thiourea and 0.9 mL of DAMO in aqueous solution were mixed thoroughly for 15 min. Second, a certain concentration of catechol was added and mixed via ultrasonication for 10 min until the solution changed to a pinkish-brown color. Last, it was moved to the reactor, where it reacted for five hours at 180 °C. The obtained crude product was dialyzed in dialysis bags with a MWCO of 1000 for 8 h, followed by freeze-drying for 36 h to obtain a purified solid sample. The prepared products were then stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for subsequent experiments.

The optical properties of the SiQDs were recorded with a TU-1901 spectrometer (Beijing Pu-Analysis) and an F-4700 fluorescence photometer (Hitachi, Ibaraki, Japan). The morphology and crystal structure were investigated via high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) (JEM-2010, JEOL, Showashima, Tokyo, Japan) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Smartlab-3, Rigaku, Akashima, Tokyo, Japan), and the elemental composition of the materials was analyzed via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo Scientific Escalab 250, Thermo Fisher, Franklin, MA, USA). In addition, the surface charge states and hydration diameters of the SiQDs and BSA were measured before and after interaction, respectively, by using a nanoparticle sizer (Zetasizer Nano ZS 90, Malvern, Worcestershire, UK).

At three different temperatures (298.15 K, 305.15 K, and 313.15 K), the fluorescence emission spectra (λem) of the BSA-SiQD systems were measured on an F-4700 fluorescence spectrometer equipped with a 1.0 cm quartz cassette; the excitation wavelength (λex) was adjusted to 280 nm while maintaining an excitation width and slit width of 10 nm each. The final spectrum was determined as the average of the data obtained from three consecutive scans. During the experiments, the concentration of BSA was set to 2.0 × 10−6 mol/L, and the concentrations of the SiQDs were 2.5 × 10−5 mol/L, 5 × 10−5 mol/L, 7.5 × 10−5 mol/L, 10 × 10−5 mol/L, 12.5 × 10−5 mol/L, 15 × 10−5 mol/L, 17.5 × 10−5 mol/L and 20 × 10−5 mol/L. All of them were dissolved in a Tris-HCl buffer solution at a concentration of 0.02 mol/L, with a pH range of 7.20–7.40.

With the same spectrometer, three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of the BSA and BSA-SiQDs systems were recorded in the excitation wavelength range of 200–350 nm and in the emission wavelength range of 200–500 nm with a 1 nm increment. During this procedure, the concentrations of SiQDs were 0, 2.5 × 10−5 mol/L, 7.5 × 10−5 mol/L, and 12.5 × 10−5 mol/L, whereas the concentration of BSA was 4.0 × 10−6 mol/L.

The synchronous fluorescence spectra of the BSA-SiQD systems were measured via the same instrument as above. For the measurement of tryptophan residues (Trp) and tyrosine residues (Tyr), Δλ was fixed at 20 and 60 nm, respectively. The scan speed was 1200 nm min−1, the photomultiplier voltage was 430 V, and the excitation and emission slit widths were 10 nm. Furthermore, the concentration ranges of both BSA and SiQDs agreed with the range of the results of the steady-state fluorescence studies, and the concentrations of both materials were compatible with the steady-state spectra. Fluorescence of SiQDs has been deducted from the experiment in this study.

The concentrations of BSA and SiQDs in the site-binding competition studies were consistent with the stated concentrations, while the concentrations of both ibuprofen and warfarin were 0.01 mol/L. After the mixture was incubated at 298.15 K for 25 min, the fluorescence spectra of the mixture were examined between 300 and 450 nm at an excitation wavelength of 280 nm.

A TU-1901 spectrometer fitted with a quartz cuvette having an optical range length of 1 cm, a scan step of 0.5 nm, and a scan range of 190 nm to 350 nm was used to acquire UV–Vis absorption spectra for the BSA and BSA-SiQD systems. The methods involved 2.0 × 10−6 mol/L BSA and 2.5 × 10−5 mol/L, 5 × 10−5 mol/L, 7.5 × 10−5 mol/L, 10 × 10−5 mol/L, 12.5 × 10−5 mol/L, 15 × 10−5 mol/L, 17.5 × 10−5 mol/L, and 20 × 10−5 mol/L SiQDs, respectively.

Applied Photophysics Ltd., Surrey, UK, provided the Chirascan circular dichroism instrument, which was used to produce the CD spectra of the BSA and BSA-SiQD systems at 298.15 K. The wavelength range was 200–260 nm, the response time was 0.5 s, and the scanning speed was 200 nm min−1. The spectra were acquired by repeatedly measuring 3 times. During this procedure, the concentration of SiQDs was 15 × 10−5 mol/L versus 25 × 10−5 mol/L, while the concentration of BSA was fixed at 6.0 × 10−6 mol/L.

For 1 h, 5 × 10−5 mol/L SiQDs were mixed with 3.0 × 10−6 mol/L BSA and incubated. Next, with a Malvern nanoparticle-size potentiostat, the water content and diameter as well as the zeta potential of the SiQD, BSA, and BSA–SiQD systems were examined. For every sample, the measurements were made three times, and the spectra were acquired by repeatedly measuring 3 times.

The generation of p-nitrophenol at 400 nm can be used to measure the impact of SiQDs on the important esterase-like activity of BSA [24]. In this experiment, 2.0 × 10−6 mol/L BSA was mixed with several concentrations of SiQDs and incubated for 25 min, after which 2.0 × 10−3 mol/L pNPA was added, and during a 4-min reaction, the absorbance values of the reaction products were measured at 400 nm. The relative esterase-like activity of BSA was determined via the following formula [25]:
(1)
Relativeesterase - l i k e a c t i v i t y
%
=
Δ
A x
Δ
A y
× 100 %
where ΔAx and ΔAy indicate the absorbance change values of pNPA within 4 min at 400 nm with the addition of the BSA–SiQD complex and with only BSA. Notably, the background absorption of the SiQDs was deduced from the assay.

BSA contains several internal disulfide bonds and chemically reactive free sulfhydryl groups, which gives BSA an important antioxidant capacity [26]. First, different quantities of SiQDs were combined with 4.0 × 10−6 mol/L BSA. Second, 0.02 mol/L DTNB reagent was added to the system, mixed, and allowed to incubate for 25 min. At 412 nm, the absorbance of the solution changed, and throughout the detection process, the background absorption of the SiQDs was also subtracted.

The effects of catechol and thiourea concentrations and various reaction times and temperatures were optimized to determine the optical characteristics of the SiQDs. As shown in Fig. 1 A and Fig. 1B, the optimal catechol content was 3.0 mg, and the optimal thiourea content was 2.0 mg. When the amount of added catechol decreased from 7.0 mg to 0.5 mg, the emission wavelength of the SiQDs gradually shifted from 527 nm to 507 nm. This is because of the change of catechol addition has a greater effect on the optical characteristics of SiQDs than does the change of thiourea content. In addition, as shown in Fig. 1C and Fig. 2 D, the ideal reaction time and temperature are 180 °C and 5 h, respectively, indicating that they are important for both the nucleation of SiQDs and the synergistic interaction of catechol and thiourea [21].

Under the optimal synthesis conditions, as shown in Fig. 2A, the obtained SiQDs exhibited good fluorescence capabilities. The inset also shows the capacity of SiQDs to emit a distinct green fluorescence when they are exposed to a 365 nm UV lamp. Fig. 2B displays the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the SiQDs. It can be inferred that the absorption peaks at 422 nm and 239 nm are unique to the SiQD structure and are caused by π–π* and n–π* leaps [27].

To investigate the optical stability of the SiQDs, their fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at different pH values, placement times and durations of UV lamp irradiation, as well as in high-salt environments. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the fluorescence intensity of the synthesized SiQDs increased as the pH increased, which may be related to the protonation and deprotonation of the surface functional groups of the SiQDs [28], however, the fluorescence intensity was relatively stable between pH = 5 and pH = 10. As shown in Fig. 3B, the effect of placement time on the optical properties of the SiQDs was tested, and a relatively stable optical intensity was maintained for up to 15 days. Fig. 3C shows the SiQDs irradiated at 365 nm UV for 1 h. After one hour of irradiation, the fluorescence intensity of the SiQDs remained high, and after approximately forty minutes, there was a slight decrease in fluorescence intensity, which was related to the bleaching property of light [29]. For the salinity tolerance test of the SiQDs, NaCl was chosen for use, and as shown in Fig. 3D, there was no significant change in fluorescence emission intensity in a 0.8 mol/L NaCl solution. In summary, the superior optical stability of the synthesized SiQDs is helpful for their use in biomedical fields.

The morphology and size of the synthesized SiQDs were analyzed via HRTEM. Fig. 4 A and Fig. 4B show that the synthesized SiQDs have good dispersion and that their particle size is distributed between 4.2 nm and 5.4 nm. Additionally, FT–IR spectroscopy was used to observe the composition of the SiQD surface functional groups. The stretching vibrations of the OH and NH bonds are linked to the absorption peaks at 3364 cm−1 and 3282 cm−1, respectively, as illustrated in Fig. 4C. [30]. The stretching and bending vibrations of the CH bond are associated with the peaks at 2936 cm−1 and 1474 cm−1, respectively [31]. The absorption peaks at 1593 cm−1 and 780 cm−1 correspond to the bending and fluctuation vibrations of NH, respectively [32]. Furthermore, it is possible to observe peaks for the CN bond at 1316 cm−1, the SiC bond at 1028 cm−1, the SiOSi bond at 1120 cm−1, and the sec–amine bond at 687 cm−1 [33]. The presence of these surface functional groups is more helpful for the good water solubility and stability of SiQDs. Fig. 4D depicts the crystalline structure of the synthesized SiQDs, and the diffraction peak at approximately 21.9 nm corresponds to the standard card, which suggests that the synthesized SiQDs have an amorphous structure, which is in agreement with the literature reports [34].

XPS was used to examine the structure or chemical bonding of the SiQDs. The elements C, N, O, Si, and S are present in the SiQDs, as shown in Fig. 5 A. Fig. 5B displays the elemental composition of the SiQDs. This figure shows that four fitted peaks correspond to CSi bonds, CC/CC bonds, CN bonds, and CO/COC bonds in the SiQDs. These peaks emerge at 284.2 eV, 284.6 eV, 285.1 eV, and 285.7 eV [35]. Fig. 5C shows the presence of NSi bonds (398.8 eV) and CNC bonds (399.4 eV), which are NSi bonds [32]. The fitted peaks for OC bonds (532.7 eV) and OSi bonds (531.3 eV and 532.1 eV) are clearly visible in Fig. 5D [36]. The presence of SiC (101.7 eV), SiN (102.1 eV) and SiO (102.7 eV) was also confirmed, as shown in the fine mapping of Si2p in Fig. 5E [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence spectroscopy studies of the interaction between SiQDs and BSA

ce:section.ce:para:
To examine how SiQDs affect the endogenous fluorescence of BSA, fluorescence spectra of the interaction system were obtained at 298.15 K. As shown in Fig. 6 , when SiQDs were added in equal proportions under 280 nm excitation, the endogenous fluorescence intensity of BSA at 340 nm gradually decreased, and the fluorescence peak position at 340 nm slightly blueshifted to 335 nm. These findings suggest that there is a relationship between SiQDs and BSA, which significantly changes the microenvironment of BSA [38]. For the quenching mechanism between the two, the Stern–Volmer equation and modified Stern–Volmer equation can be used [39,40]:
(2)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
Sv
[ Q ]
= 1 +
K q
τ 0
Q
(3)
F 0
Δ F
=
1
f a
K a
[ Q ]
+
1
f a
where [Q] is the concentration of SiQDs, Ksv is the Stern–Volmer burst constant, Kq is the bimolecular burst rate constant, and τ0 is the fluorescence lifetime of BSA in the absence of SiQDs. Furthermore, F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity of BSA with SiQDs and without SiQDs, respectively. The solvent accessible to the molar fraction of fluorophores is denoted by fa , the associative binding constant is represented by Ka , and the difference in fluorescence intensity of the fluorescent molecules before and after the addition of SiQDs is shown by ΔF.

The Stern–Volmer quenching curves and the modified Stern–Volmer quenching curves (shown in Fig. 7 A, B) were obtained after linear fitting, and the corresponding fitting parameters are listed in Table 1 . The values of Ksv , Kq , and Ka are negatively associated with temperature, which indicates that the main type of quenching mechanism during the combination of the two is a static quenching mechanism [41].

The acquisition of binding constants as well as the number of binding sites is important for assessing the degree of reactivity of SiQDs when they interact with BSA, which can be calculated from the Scatchard equation [42]:
(4)
log
F 0
- F
F
= l o g
K b
+ n l o g
[ Q ]
where Kb is the binding constant and n is the number of binding sites. F and F0 have the same meaning as above.

As shown in Fig. 7C and Table 2 , the n of the BSA-SiQD systems does not change much among the three temperatures, which enables both systems to always have at least one binding site during the binding process. The magnitude of Kb , on the other hand, decreases as the temperature increases, which is consist with that the type of quenching mechanism of the two is a static quenching mechanism.

The binding process between proteins and nanomaterials is driven by a variety of factors [43], and Fig. 7D and Table 3 provide detailed information about the thermodynamic parameters during the interaction between SiQDs and BSA, where the binding of SiQDs to BSA is spontaneous (ΔG < 0) in the interaction process and accompanied by exothermic reactions and entropy reduction (ΔH < 0, ΔS < 0). van der Waals forces and hydrogen bonding forces are thus the primary forces responsible for the binding of SiQDs and BSA during contact. This may be related to the notion that the surface of the SiQDs possesses large amounts of OH and NH2. In addition, the absolute value of ΔS is much smaller than that of ΔH in the interaction between SiQDs and BSA, which indicates that ΔH dominates the interaction between SiQDs and BSA.

BSA is composed mainly of an α-helix and irregularly coiled polypeptide structure, which contains three structural domains: structural domain I (1–193), structural domain II (194–384), and structural domain III (385–583), each of which has two substructural domains. The primary binding sites for BSA in subdomains IIA and IIIA are Site I and Site II, respectively [44]. Warfarin and ibuprofen are useful probes that can be utilized to identify the major binding sites during the binding process of SiQDs to BSA to achieve site-specific binding [45]. If the site-specific probes overlap with the binding sites of the SiQDs, the Ka value of their probe-BSA–SiQD ternary system will change significantly. As demonstrated in Fig. 8 A and 8B, the addition of warfarin altered both the position and intensity of the BSA fluorescence peak, whereas the addition of ibuprofen resulted in a decrease in the BSA fluorescence intensity. The modified Stern–Volmer plots of the systems before and after the addition of ibuprofen and warfarin are displayed in Fig. 8C, and Table 4 displays the specific parameters. When warfarin and ibuprofen were present in the BSA-SiQD systems, the Ka value decreased by 50.32 % and 12.57 %, respectively, which indicated that the SiQDs mainly occupied Site I during their interaction with BSA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy study of the interaction between SiQDs and BSA
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–visible absorption spectra of BSA before and after the addition of SiQDs are displayed in Fig. 9 A. Various concentrations of SiQDs were used to interact with BSA (Fig. 9B) to investigate the changes located at 278 nm (characteristic absorption peak of aromatic amino acids). As shown in Fig. 9A, when the SiQDs interacted with BSA, the UV–visible absorption spectrum of BSA significantly differed from the UV–visible absorption spectrum of the [BSA-SiQDs]-SiQDs. As shown in Fig. 9B, with the addition of SiQDs, BSA clearly enhances color at 278 nm, which suggests that the two form a base complex rather than just undergoing intermolecular collisions [46]. Furthermore, the slightly altered peak location indicates that the addition of SiQDs may have had some effect on the Trp and Tyr microenvironmental alterations in BSA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of structural changes in the interaction of SiQDs with BSA
Synchronous fluorescence spectroscopy study of the interaction between SiQDs and BSA
ce:section.ce:para: Scanning the synchrotron fluorescence spectra facilitates the identification of changes in the microenvironmental conditions of the Tyr and Trp residues in the interaction of the BSA–SiQDs system [47]. Fig. 10 displays the synchronous fluorescence spectra of the BSA–SiQD systems. As the amount of SiQDs increased, both the fluorescence intensity of the Tyr and Trp residues in BSA dramatically decreased. Moreover, the quenching effect of SiQDs on Trp residues is significantly greater than the degree of quenching of Tyr residues. Furthermore, the presence of SiQDs increased the degree of peptide chain unfolding and decreased the hydrophobicity of the microenvironment surrounding the Tyr and Trp residues in BSA, altering its original tertiary structure. As the concentration of SiQDs increased, the position of the characteristic peak of the Tyr residue of BSA clearly redshifted, whereas the position of the characteristic peak of the Trp residue slightly redshifted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Three-dimensional fluorescence spectroscopy study of the interaction between SiQDs and BSA
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 11 displays the three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of the reactions between BSA and various concentrations of SiQDs. The π–π* leaps of aromatic amino acids and the π–π* leaps of the peptide backbone structure of BSA are indicated by peaks 1 and 2, respectively, and are intimately linked to the modifications in the secondary and tertiary structures of BSA [48]. The analysis of the data in Fig. 11 and Table 5 clearly reveals that the incorporation of SiQDs caused a differential reduction in the intensity of the two distinct peaks of BSA, accompanied by a commensurate increase in their respective Stokes shifts. The above results indicate that the interaction between SiQDs and BSA affected the secondary and tertiary structures of BSA to varying degrees. This led to an increase in the degree of unfolding of the BSA polypeptide, as well as an increase in the hydrophilicity of the microenvironment surrounding the aromatic amino acids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Circular dichroism (CD) study of the interaction between SiQDs and BSA
ce:section.ce:para: The CD spectral profile of BSA within the far-ultraviolet spectral region, spanning the 190 nm to 260 nm wavelength, is intricately correlated with its secondary structural conformation [49]. Fig. 12 A displays the CD spectra of BSA in combination with SiQDs. BSA has a distinct negative peak at 208 and 222 nm, and the presence of SiQDs increases the strength of this peak. This finding implies that the addition of SiQDs reduces the α-helix content of BSA. Fig. 12B displays the percentage composition of the BSA secondary structure as determined by the BESTSEL online software. When SiQDs were present at concentrations of 0, 15 × 10−5 mol/L (BSA–SiQDs-1) and 25 × 10−5 mol/L (BSA–SiQDs-2), the α-helix content of BSA decreased from 71.1 % to 69.4 % and 63.6 %, its β-folding decreased from 4.7 % to 2.1 % and 1.3 %, and its irregular curls and other structures increased from 17.1 % to 21 % and 29.1 %, suggesting that the incorporation of SiQDs increased the extent of the spatial structure of BSA and increased the hydrophilicity of its aromatic amino acid microenvironment, which is consistent with the above findings.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of the hydration diameter and surface charge
ce:section.ce:para: Dynamic light scattering can be used to determine how the surface charge state and particle size of SiQDs change when they interact with BSA [50]. As shown in Fig. 13 A, the hydration diameters of BSA and SiQDs are 210.3 and 154.5 nm, respectively, which are much larger than the size determined by TEM because of the small particle size of the SiQDs and thus are highly susceptible to aggregation in the PBS buffer solution. When the SiQDs were combined with BSA, the hydration diameter reached 345.9 nm, which further indicated that the combination of the two formed a tight complex. Under physiological conditions, the zeta potential for BSA is −20.9 mv (pI = 4.7), whereas those for SiQDs and BSA-SiQDs are −12.2 mv and −16.1 mv, respectively, as depicted in Fig. 13B. In addition to the results of the fluorescence experiments, it is possible to speculate that van der Waals and hydrogen-bonding forces—rather than electrostatic forces—are the primary forces involved in the bonding process between SiQDs and BSA because their surface potentials both have negative potentials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of SiQD and BSA interactions on important physiological functions
ce:section.ce:para: It is commonly recognized that BSA has both hydrolytic and catalytic esterase activity, with the IIA/IIIA substructural domain accounting for the majority of its esterase activity functional region [51]. The effect of SiQDs on the esterase activity of BSA can be examined via UV absorption spectroscopy by measuring the absorbance of the nitrophenol produced by BSA from p-nitrophenyl acetate (p-NPA) at 400 nm. Fig. 14 A shows how the addition of SiQDs affected the esterase activity of BSA. This figure shows that over the course of four minutes, the concentration of SiQDs increased, and importantly, for biomedical applications, the esterase activity increased in a concentration-dependent manner.

With a free sulfhydryl group at position 34 of the peptide chain and 583 amino acid residues, 35 of which are cysteines and form 17 disulfide bonds, BSA possesses significant antioxidant activity [52]. The creation of a yellow-colored material, the 2-nitro-5-thiobenzoate anion (TNB), and a mixed disulfide molecule (R–S–TNB) are the outcomes of the reactions between the free sulfhydryl group (R–SH) and 5,5-dithio-bis-(2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB) [53]. The effect of SiQD addition on the antioxidant capacity of BSA is depicted in Fig. 14B. At low concentrations, the addition of SiQDs had virtually no influence on the antioxidant effect of BSA, and as the concentration of SiQDs increased, the antioxidant capacity decreased. Combined with the results of the conformational change experiments described above, we hypothesized that the high concentration of SiQDs caused a substantial increase in the degree of BSA unfolding during the interaction. This increase in unfolding likely increased the exposure of free sulfhydryl groups, thereby increasing their contact area with DTNB, ultimately manifesting as an increased antioxidant capacity.

In this work, green fluorescence-emitting SiQDs were successfully synthesized by exploiting the synergistic effect between catechol and thiourea bi-reducers, and the interaction mechanism between SiQDs and BSA was investigated via multispectral methods as well as dynamic light scattering methods. It can be concluded from the results that SiQDs are closer to Site I of BSA in the binding process and form a tight complex with BSA by static quenching. In the binding process, van der Waals and hydrogen bonding forces are the main forces, accompanied by exothermic reactions and entropy reduction. Moreover, with the addition of SiQDs, the α-helix and β-folding of BSA changed to other structures, such as irregular curling, which caused an increase in the degree of stretching of BSA and an increase in the hydrophilicity of the microenvironments around the Tyr residues and Trp residues. With the addition of SiQDs, the antioxidant capacity and esterase activity of BSA increased and were positively correlated with the concentration. These results provide not only insight into the binding interactions between green fluorescence-emitting SiQDs and BSA but also useful information for their potential applications in biological fields.

Meng Ji: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Yanan Qiang: Methodology, Validation. Si Li: Resources, Writing – original draft. Chunyuan Tian: Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Xuming Zhuang: Validation, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Shengda Qi: Writing – review & editing. Feng Luan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Writing – review & editing.
This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province in China (ZR2021MB024) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (21778047, 21675138, 21705139).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
